questions.question: What is polypharmacy typically defined as?
options: A. Taking any prescription medication
B. Taking five or more medications
C. Taking only over-the-counter medications
D. Taking medications from more than one pharmacy
questions.rationale: Polypharmacy is typically defined as taking five or more medications, increasing risk of interactions and side effects.

questions.question: What medication classes carry particular fall risk in older adults?
options: A. Sedatives/hypnotics and some psychiatric medications
B. Vitamins and supplements
C. Topical creams
D. Eye drops
questions.rationale: Sedatives, hypnotics, and certain psychiatric medications increase fall risk through sedation or impaired balance.

questions.question: What medication classes carry particular bleeding risk after trauma?
options: A. Antibiotics
B. Anticoagulants and antiplatelet medications
C. Antihistamines
D. Antacids
questions.rationale: Anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications increase bleeding risk after trauma, including minor head injury.

questions.question: Should a geriatric fall be assumed to be purely mechanical without further investigation?
options: A. Yes, falls in older adults are always mechanical
B. No — falls can be the presenting symptom of a medical emergency (arrhythmia, stroke, hypoglycemia, etc.)
C. Only if the patient is under 75
D. Only if there were no witnesses
questions.rationale: A fall should not be assumed purely mechanical; it may be the presenting symptom of an underlying medical emergency.

questions.question: What should be asked about the circumstances of a geriatric fall?
options: A. Witnessed vs. unwitnessed, preceding symptoms, ability to recall the event
B. Only the exact time of day
C. Only whether the patient was wearing shoes
D. Nothing beyond confirming the fall occurred
questions.rationale: A thorough fall history includes whether it was witnessed, any preceding warning symptoms, and the patient's recall of the event.

questions.question: Why might occult injury be more likely to be missed in older adults after a fall?
options: A. Older adults may have a blunted pain response
B. Older adults always report pain accurately
C. Occult injury cannot occur in geriatric patients
D. Older adults are less likely to have any injury
questions.rationale: A blunted pain response in older adults can mask significant injury, making thorough exam important regardless of reported pain.

questions.question: Why is anticoagulant use plus head injury (even minor) a significant field red flag?
options: A. It carries meaningfully elevated risk of intracranial hemorrhage with possible delayed symptom onset
B. Anticoagulants have no effect on head injury risk
C. It only matters for injuries above the neck in adults under 50
D. This combination is not clinically significant
questions.rationale: Anticoagulated patients face elevated intracranial hemorrhage risk after even minor head trauma, and symptoms can be delayed.

questions.question: Does a normal initial neurologic exam exclude a developing intracranial bleed in an anticoagulated patient after head injury?
options: A. Yes, a normal exam rules out any bleed
B. No — a normal initial exam does not exclude a developing bleed
C. Only if the patient is under 60
D. Only if no anticoagulant is involved
questions.rationale: Intracranial hemorrhage in an anticoagulated patient can develop with delayed onset, so an initially normal exam does not exclude it.

questions.question: What general findings may raise concern for elder abuse or neglect?
options: A. Injury-mechanism inconsistency, neglect signs, unexplained/patterned bruising, controlling caregiver behavior
B. A clean, well-maintained living environment
C. A caregiver who welcomes private conversation with the patient
D. Consistent, corroborated injury history
questions.rationale: These findings are recognized general categories that may raise concern for elder abuse or neglect, similar to pediatric maltreatment recognition.

questions.question: Is any single finding diagnostic of elder abuse?
options: A. Yes, bruising alone is diagnostic
B. No — no single finding is diagnostic; the EMS role is recognition and objective documentation
C. Yes, but only in patients with dementia
D. Yes, if the caregiver seems nervous
questions.rationale: As with pediatric maltreatment, no single finding confirms elder abuse; providers document objectively and let appropriate professionals evaluate.

questions.question: Are EMS providers mandatory reporters of suspected elder abuse?
options: A. No, reporting is optional
B. Yes, in essentially all jurisdictions
C. Only if the patient requests it
D. Only social workers are mandatory reporters
questions.rationale: EMS providers are mandatory reporters of suspected elder abuse in essentially all jurisdictions.

questions.question: What is the correct EMS approach when elder abuse is suspected?
options: A. Confront the caregiver directly to obtain a confession
B. Provide standard care, document objectively, communicate calmly and non-accusatorially, and report through appropriate channels
C. Refuse to treat the patient until the situation is clarified
D. Make a definitive determination of abuse before transport
questions.rationale: The EMS role is recognition and reporting, not investigation or confrontation — mirroring the approach to pediatric maltreatment.

questions.question: A caregiver insists on answering every question for an alert, oriented geriatric patient and will not leave the room. What should this prompt?
options: A. No concern — caregivers should always answer for patients
B. Consideration as a potential concern-raising finding, to be documented objectively rather than confronted directly
C. Immediate accusation of the caregiver
D. Refusal to provide care until the caregiver leaves
questions.rationale: A controlling caregiver dynamic is a recognized concern-raising finding that should be documented objectively rather than acted on with direct confrontation.

questions.question: A geriatric patient on warfarin reports a minor bump to the head with no current symptoms. What is the correct approach?
options: A. No transport needed since there are no symptoms
B. Recognize the elevated intracranial hemorrhage risk from anticoagulation despite the minor mechanism and current absence of symptoms, and communicate this clearly at handoff
C. Assume the medication is irrelevant to this injury
D. Delay documentation of the anticoagulant use
questions.rationale: Anticoagulant use with head injury — even seemingly minor and currently asymptomatic — carries meaningful bleed risk that should be evaluated and clearly communicated.

questions.question: A patient reports feeling dizzy and having heart palpitations just before falling. How should this fall be approached differently from a simple trip?
options: A. Identically, since all falls are treated the same regardless of preceding symptoms
B. As a potential medical cause of the fall (e.g., arrhythmia), warranting cardiac evaluation in addition to injury assessment
C. As purely a trauma case with no cardiac relevance
D. As unrelated to any medical condition
questions.rationale: Preceding symptoms like dizziness and palpitations suggest the fall may be a symptom of an underlying medical cause (e.g., arrhythmia), requiring broader evaluation beyond just the traumatic injury.
